title: Top 5 Android 17 Features: I Swear It's New!

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: All right, so I do one of these videos for every new Android version. Top five features for what to expect, so you have a better idea of what will be coming to your phone, if it's a pixel, very soon, if it's not a pixel, someday.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: But we all know these aren't massive generational overhaul updates anymore. Those days are few and far between. Instead, now it's more usually like the small cosmetic stuff and little tweaks and quality of life things here and there. Back in the day, that would have all been like a dot-one update.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: But since it's 2026, it's an entirely new Android version, Android 17: Cinnamon Bun.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: Now, I know what you're thinking. Hold on a second, wait. What? Did the, did the names come back? When did the names come back? The names aren't back, but I'm trying to bring them back.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: Do you remember when every new Android version was named after an alphabetical dessert and the biggest surprise of every IO was going to be them unveiling what the dessert name would be?

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: I mean, we had classics like Cupcake, Donut, Eclair, Froyo, Gingerbread. And then as the letters went on, they got more and more playful with it: Nougat, Oreo, Pie.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: But then they got to Q, and so, you know, I could understand not coming up with a dessert name just for Q. But then after that, they just went straight to the numbers. They stopped the names, they just went Android 13, Android 14, Android 15.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: But internally, Google has actually kept doing the internal dessert code names, and you can look on the Wikipedia, you can see after Q, there was Red Velvet Cake, Snow Cone, Tiramisu, Upside Down Cake, Vanilla Ice Cream.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: And then I guess they just skipped W, X, Y, Z and A for some reason, and now here we are at Cinnamon Bun.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: Android 16 was Baklava, so actually if you get any system notification, that's what this little glyph here was meant to be.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: And so sure enough, if you get any system notification in Android 17, there's a little cinnamon bun. Proof that somebody at Google still cares about this. Bring back the public dessert names, Google. It's more fun this way.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: Anyway, these are my top five features on the publicly available Android 17 on my pixel, in my order.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: So number five: screen reactions are a built-in feature now. You go in and start a screen recording, and now you can just tick the show selfie camera option and you get live keyed-in a version of yourself that you can move around and make bigger, smaller, and then record on the spot, which is very convenient.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: Now, I'll be honest, I never made these types of videos, so I don't know if this is sherlocking some other big app people were all using to do this, or if they've actually built it into Instagram or TikTok at this point, I'm sure they did. But hey, it just got that much easier, so nicely done, Google.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: Now number four is very cool. It's the new game mode on foldables. So on Pixel Fold and Z Fold and all these other foldables that get Android 17, you'll be able to play games where half the screen is the game and the other half is an on-screen gamepad.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: And I'm not even that much of a phone gamer, but that is really cool, not gonna lie.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: Now number three is the bubbles and the bubble bar. And I really like saying bubble bar, sorry.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: I'm gonna say it a lot but the bubble bar is new. So basically any app you can now launch straight into a floating window that can minimize into a bubble at any time. This actually itself isn't new you could do this with some messaging related apps for a while, but what's new is the ability to have up to four bubbles open at once of different apps and then kind of treat it like a multitasking hub over the top of whatever else is going on in the background. But this bubble bar can be very useful. Imagine taking notes over the top of a YouTube video playing. I imagine I'm not the only freak that actually writes notes instead of having like Gemini summarize everything for me cause the bubble bar seems perfect for that.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: Anyway, separate but related feature, the new screen recording UI, I think kind of looks like it was inspired by the bubble bar. With the new pill at the top. It's not actually one of the bubbles but does feel thematically in line. Makes you think.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: So then number two is this whole collection of what I'm going to call aesthetic tweaks. And again it's not some big overhaul this isn't like a new liquid glass or anything crazy like that, but it is a good collection of things where it feels like somebody is going through and being very thoughtful about little things that could be better or that should be a little different.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: Like I like that you can hide app labels now on the home screen for a super clean look in the stock launcher. I feel like that's long overdue.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: I also like that there's a bit less empty white space between options in some of the more buried settings. Like that's a subtle thing but it's nicely considered. It was getting to be a little bit much before.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: And then there's actually a little bit of transparency added here now. Not full on liquid glass levels again but just more adding it to more places for consistency. So the app drawer already had it, the quick settings already had it, now the widget picker has it as well so you can see a little bit of that wallpaper peaking through there as you pick your widgets.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: You can choose exactly which apps automatically open in the expanded dark mode theme now instead of just everything, you can have certain apps that always come up in dark mode.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: And then it's such a small thing but when you finish going through notifications in Android 16 it would just have a little text that says, No notifications. In Android 17 it says, You're all caught up and gives you a nice little trophy, which is nice.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: Now before we get to number one I of course need to show you the new Android 17 Easter egg which this year is an incredibly complicated puzzle. So you do the classic five taps on Android 17 and then it takes you into this weird constellation type thing with a ring of stars. And I stared at this for hours trying to figure out what does it mean, what types of symbols do I have to draw here, what sort of patterns are going to make this work, and uh no just kidding, you basically just connect them all together in literally any way and then the Android 17 logo pops up. And then you hold that down for a few seconds to get into the same Easter egg as previous years which is this little Asteroids game where you pilot this little spaceship around. It's a fun time.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: So then, the number one feature of Android 17 is that they've finally separated the Wi-Fi

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: and mobile data toggles in quick settings. Thank God. I don't know why, but a couple years ago they decided for some reason to merge them together into one internet toggle that you had to click into and then click again to toggle either Wi-Fi or mobile data. So finally in Android 17, they heard us, they listened to us and they separated back out a Wi-Fi toggle and a mobile data toggle in quick settings. You can tap just the icon to turn it on or off or tap the words to enter that full menu and connect to different networks. It's about damn time they brought this back.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: Now obviously this isn't the number one biggest change to Android 17, but it does feel like it's part of a nice theme of Google actually listening to users and just making little tweak type things at the surface level that make Android nicer to use.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: Like here's another one. In Android 16, there's a permanent search button on the bottom of your home screen. You can't get rid of it. Now they had this cool customization that let you change transparency and theme color of the widget, but that was only if you added a second search widget to your home screen, it would customize that one, which makes no sense. Why would anyone add a second search widget? Why can't you customize the first one that you can't get rid of? Well in Android 17, now you long press it and you actually can. You can change the transparency and customize that first one straight away. Still can't remove it, but that's better than nothing.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: And so there's so many other little things like that. There's slightly bouncier, more exaggerated animations in quick settings and in multitasking and big little performance tweaks here and there throughout. There's also more stuff coming too, like the ability to make new widgets from scratch using natural language when they add that, that's going to be sick, I think. And Rambler, which they talked about at IO, which is going to be even better speech to text and Pause Point as well, which is another digital wellbeing tool.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: Stuff like this makes me want to switch to the Pixel again and take the plunge before I come back to my senses and remember the type of pain that that can turn into. I am once again hoping for Tensor to take a massive leap forward with the next generation of Pixel phones, but I've been hurt before, so I'm not going to cross my fingers.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: Anyway, if you just got Android 17 to update the inside of your Pixel, then it might be time to do the same with the outside, which leads to today's sponsor dbrand. Their summer sale just went live and you can get up to 60% off various things and a ton of other deals. Starting with the cases, they're all $10 off, no code required. The Grip is, as you can imagine, super grippy and customizable with a ton of different skins. The Ghost case is literally guaranteed to never yellow or they'll replace it for free. And the Tank is the rugged one you'll either love or hate. But the real deal is when you bundle it with one of their idiot-proof screen protectors, their words not mine, to get $20 off the entire bundle. I've been using one since they first got released. You can barely tell it's on there, but a big part of that is just how easy it is to install. Takes like 10 seconds and it also comes with two in the box. And if you spend more than 50 bucks, they'll toss in a deck of dbrand-themed playing cards for free. And there's a ton of other deals going on.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: on a site right now like 55 bucks off the Killswitch and I kid you not 0 dollars off the new Steam machine companion cube.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: It's classic. I'll leave a link down in the description so you can check everything out for yourself. Thanks for watching and I'll cinnamon bun you later. Peace.

highlights: Android updates have transitioned from major generational overhauls to incremental "quality of life" improvements and cosmetic refinements.
A significant user-requested change is the re-separation of Wi-Fi and mobile data toggles in Quick Settings, undoing the previous merged "Internet" button.
The new "bubble bar" feature facilitates advanced multitasking by allowing up to four apps to be launched into floating windows that can be minimized or moved over other content.
Android 17 optimizes foldable devices with a dedicated game mode that displays the game on one half of the screen and an on-screen gamepad on the other.
New aesthetic controls allow for a cleaner interface by letting users hide app labels on the home screen and customize the transparency of the permanent search bar.
summary:
In this video, Marques Brownlee reviews the key features of Android 17, internally codenamed "Cinnamon Bun." He notes that modern Android updates have shifted away from massive overhauls toward iterative "quality of life" improvements and aesthetic refinements. A major theme of this version is Google’s apparent willingness to listen to user feedback, evidenced by the return of separate Wi-Fi and mobile data toggles in the Quick Settings—a reversal of a controversial change made years prior.

The review highlights several functional additions, including a "bubble bar" that allows for advanced multitasking with up to four floating app windows and a new game mode for foldables that transforms half the screen into a dedicated gamepad. Other notable features include a built-in screen recording tool with a selfie-camera overlay for "reaction" videos and a series of aesthetic tweaks, such as the ability to hide app labels on the home screen and a more customizable permanent search bar.

Beyond current features, Brownlee touches on the new "constellation" Easter egg and upcoming AI-driven tools like natural language widget creation and improved speech-to-text. While he remains impressed by the software refinements on the Pixel, he expresses his perennial hope that Google’s future hardware and Tensor chips will eventually match the polish of the Android operating system.
